steps.title: Identify the diagram components
steps.content:
To sketch an energy profile (also known as an enthalpy profile), we need to set up a coordinate system. This is a key skill from **Topic R1.1.3: Energy profiles**. 

We need to draw two separate graphs, one for each reaction type. Every diagram must include:
1. A labeled **y-axis** representing the energy state of the chemicals.
2. A labeled **x-axis** representing the progress of the reaction.

highlights.text: Label the y-axis as potential energy/enthalpy and the x-axis as reaction coordinate/progress.

steps.title: Set up the axes
steps.content:
For both reactions, we start by drawing the axes. 
- The **y-axis** should be labeled as **Enthalpy ($H$)** or **Potential Energy**.
- The **x-axis** should be labeled as **Reaction Progress** or **Reaction Coordinate**.

This first step ensures you secure the mark for proper formatting.

steps.title: Sketch the exothermic profile
steps.content:
In an **exothermic reaction**, energy is released to the surroundings. This means the products have less energy than the reactants. 

- Draw a horizontal line for the **reactants** at a higher level.
- Draw a horizontal line for the **products** at a lower level.
- Connect them with a curve that goes up first (representing the activation energy) and then drops down to the product level.

Since the system loses energy, the change in enthalpy is negative:
$$\Delta H < 0$$

steps.title: Sketch the endothermic profile
steps.content:
In an **endothermic reaction**, energy is absorbed from the surroundings. This means the products have more energy than the reactants.

- Draw a horizontal line for the **reactants** at a lower level.
- Draw a horizontal line for the **products** at a higher level.
- Again, connect them with a curve that peaks above the product level.

Since the system gains energy, the change in enthalpy is positive:
$$\Delta H > 0$$

steps.title: Label the enthalpy change
steps.content:
The final step for full marks is to label the enthalpy change, $\Delta H$. 

On both diagrams, $\Delta H$ is shown as a vertical arrow starting from the **reactant energy level** and ending at the **product energy level**. 

As seen in your data booklet, the formula for this change is:
$$\Delta H = H_{\text{products}} - H_{\text{reactants}}$$

In the exothermic diagram, the arrow points **down**, while in the endothermic diagram, the arrow points **up**.

parts.content: Explain why some endothermic reactions can still occur spontaneously.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that endothermic reactions can occur spontaneously if they result in an increase in disorder/entropy ($"\Delta S > 0$"). <marks>M1</marks>
- Explain that $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$ must be negative for a reaction to be spontaneous, which occurs if $T\Delta S > \Delta H$. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Define spontaneity with Gibbs
steps.content:
To understand why a reaction happens on its own, we look at the **Gibbs free energy change** ($ΔG$). In IB Chemistry, a reaction is considered spontaneous only if the value of $ΔG$ is negative ($ΔG < 0$). This is calculated using the equation from your data booklet:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

steps.title: Analyze the endothermic term
steps.content:
The question specifies that the reaction is **endothermic**. This means that the enthalpy change ($ΔH$) is positive ($+ \Delta H$). 

Looking at our equation, a positive $ΔH$ value actually works against spontaneity because it tends to make $\Delta G$ positive. For the reaction to still occur spontaneously, something else must overcome this positive value.

steps.title: The role of entropy
steps.content:
The "driving force" for endothermic spontaneous reactions is an **increase in disorder**, also known as entropy ($ΔS$). 

If a reaction results in an increase in entropy, then $\Delta S$ is positive ($\Delta S > 0$). This is the first requirement to earn marks (M1). Because the equation subtracts the entropy term ($- T\Delta S$), a positive $\Delta S$ will help lower the overall value of $\Delta G$.

steps.title: Comparing TΔS and ΔH
steps.content:
For $\Delta G$ to end up being negative, the magnitude of the $T\Delta S$ term must be larger than the magnitude of the $\Delta H$ term. 

When the temperature ($T$) is high enough or the increase in disorder is significant enough that **$T\Delta S > \Delta H$**, the result of $\Delta H - T\Delta S$ becomes negative. This makes the reaction spontaneous (A1).

steps.title: Define the Second Law
steps.content:
According to the Second Law of Thermodynamics, a reaction is only spontaneous if the total entropy of the universe ($\\Delta S_{total}$) increases. This total entropy is the sum of the entropy change of the system and its surroundings:

$$\\Delta S_{total} = \\Delta S_{system} + \\Delta S_{surroundings} > 0$$

steps.title: Analyze the surroundings
steps.content:
In an **endothermic** reaction, the system absorbs heat from the surroundings. This loss of heat causes the particles in the surroundings to move less, which decreases their entropy.

The entropy change of the surroundings is calculated as:

$$\\Delta S_{surroundings} = -\\frac{\\Delta H_{system}}{T}$$

Since $\\Delta H$ is positive for endothermic reactions, $\\Delta S_{surroundings}$ will be negative.

steps.title: Requirement for system entropy
steps.content: For the total entropy ($\\Delta S_{total}$) to remain positive despite the decrease in the surroundings' entropy, the reaction must result in a significant increase in the entropy of the system ($\\Delta S > 0$). This typically occurs when there is an increase in the number of particles or a change in state (e.g., solid to liquid).

steps.title: Link to Gibbs Energy
steps.content:
We can see this relationship in the Gibbs free energy equation from the data booklet:

$$\\Delta G^{\\ominus}=\\Delta H^{\\ominus}-T \\Delta S^{\\ominus}$$

For a reaction to be spontaneous, $\\Delta G$ must be negative. In an endothermic reaction where $\\Delta H$ is positive, the reaction only occurs if the $T\\Delta S$ term is large enough to outweigh $\\Delta H$. This happens when:

$$T\\Delta S > \\Delta H$$

steps.title: Define spontaneity requirement
steps.content:
To understand why a reaction occurs spontaneously, we look at the **Gibbs free energy change** ($\Delta G$). For any process to be spontaneous, the overall change in free energy must be negative ($$\Delta G < 0$$).

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.4: Entropy and spontaneity (HL)**. Even if a reaction is endothermic (absorbs heat), it can still be spontaneous if other factors make $\Delta G$ negative.

steps.title: The Gibbs equation
steps.content:
The relationship between enthalpy ($\Delta H$), entropy ($\Delta S$), and temperature ($T$) is given by the Gibbs free energy equation in your data booklet:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} - T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

In an **endothermic** reaction, the enthalpy change is positive ($$\Delta H > 0$$). This term alone would make $\Delta G$ positive, which prevents spontaneity. Therefore, the second term ($$-T\Delta S$$) must be large enough to outweigh the positive enthalpy.

steps.title: Analyze the graph
steps.content:
If we treat the Gibbs equation as a linear function of temperature ($$y = mx + c$$), where $$y = \Delta G$$ and $$x = T$$:

$$\Delta G = (-\Delta S)T + \Delta H$$

*   The **y-intercept** is $$\Delta H$$. Since the reaction is endothermic, the graph starts at a positive value on the y-axis.
*   The **slope** of the line is $$\mathbf{-\Delta S}$$.

For the line to eventually cross into the negative region ($$\Delta G < 0$$) as temperature increases, the slope must be negative. This only happens if the **entropy change is positive** ($$\Delta S > 0$$), representing an increase in disorder.

steps.title: Identify the crossover point
steps.content:
As the temperature ($T$) increases, the value of the term $$-T\Delta S$$ becomes more negative. Eventually, at a high enough temperature, the magnitude of the entropy term exceeds the enthalpy term:

$$T\Delta S > \Delta H$$

At this point, the graph crosses the x-axis, $$\Delta G$$ becomes negative, and the endothermic reaction becomes spontaneous.

steps.title: Final summary for marks
steps.content:
To secure both marks on the exam, your explanation should include:

1.  **Entropy Increase:** State that the reaction must result in an increase in disorder or entropy ($$\Delta S > 0$$).
2.  **Magnitude Comparison:** Explain that at high temperatures, the term $$T\Delta S$$ becomes larger than $$\Delta H$$, making $$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$ negative.

parts.content: Describe the role of activation energy in determining reaction feasibility.
parts.markscheme:
- Describe activation energy as the minimum energy required to initiate a reaction by breaking bonds in reactants. <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Define activation energy
steps.content: Activation energy ($E_a$) is the minimum amount of energy required to **initiate** a chemical reaction. It acts as an energy barrier that particles must overcome during a collision for a reaction to occur.

steps.title: The role in initiation
steps.content: For a reaction to be feasible (to actually happen), the reactants must reach this energy threshold to **break the existing chemical bonds**. Once these bonds are broken, the atoms can rearrange to form the products. Even if a reaction is exothermic and releases energy overall, it still requires this initial "kick" to start.

steps.title: Define activation energy
steps.content: In chemistry, **activation energy** ($E_a$) is the minimum energy that colliding particles must possess for a reaction to occur. It serves as an energy barrier that prevents every collision from resulting in a reaction.

steps.title: Breaking reactant bonds
steps.content: From the **bond-breaking perspective**, the role of activation energy is to provide the specific amount of energy needed to break or weaken the chemical bonds within the reactant molecules. This is a prerequisite for any chemical change because the atoms must be freed from their original arrangements before they can rearrange to form new products.

steps.title: Determining feasibility
steps.content: For a reaction to be feasible at a given temperature, a sufficient number of particles must have kinetic energy equal to or greater than the activation energy. If the $E_a$ is too high relative to the available energy, the bonds will not break, and the reaction will not initiate.

steps.title: Recall Collision Theory
steps.content:
To understand reaction feasibility, we look at **Topic R2.2.2: Collision Theory**. For any reaction to occur, reactant particles must collide with each other. However, not every collision results in a reaction. Two conditions must be met:
1. Particles must collide with the correct **orientation**.
2. Particles must collide with **sufficient energy** to overcome the energy barrier.

steps.title: Define Activation Energy ($E_a$)
steps.content:
The specific amount of energy required for a successful collision is called the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

In terms of feasibility, the activation energy is the **minimum energy** required to initiate a reaction. This energy is used to **break the existing chemical bonds** in the reactant molecules so that new bonds can form to create products.

highlights.text: Describe activation energy as the minimum energy required to initiate a reaction by breaking bonds in reactants.

steps.title: Link to Feasibility
steps.content:
The role of activation energy in feasibility is acting as a **barrier**. 

Even if a reaction is energetically favorable overall (like the exothermic one mentioned in the question), it will not start unless the colliding particles possess kinetic energy equal to or greater than the $E_a$. This explains why some reactions need a spark or heat to get them going, even if they eventually proceed on their own.

highlights.text: Describe the role of activation energy in determining reaction feasibility.

steps.title: Final Answer Formulation
steps.content:
To earn the mark, your description should focus on the "threshold" nature of this energy.

**Answer:** Activation energy is the minimum amount of energy that colliding particles must possess to initiate a reaction by breaking the bonds in the reactants.

parts.content:
The decomposition of barium azide, $\text{Ba(N}_3)_2\text{(s)}$, is endothermic and spontaneous. The process follows the balanced equation shown below.

$$\text{Ba(N}_3)_2\text{(s)} \rightarrow \text{Ba(s)} + 3\text{N}_2\text{(g)}$$

Predict the entropy change for this reaction and justify your answer.
parts.markscheme:
- State that the entropy change is positive ($\Delta S > 0$). <marks>A1</marks>
- Justify that the decomposition forms 3 moles of gas from a solid reactant, significantly increasing disorder. <marks>R1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Analyze states of matter
steps.content:
To predict the entropy change ($\Delta S$), we first look at the states of matter for everything in the balanced equation. Entropy is a measure of the disorder or randomness of a system. 

In this reaction:
$$\text{Ba(N}_3)_2\text{(s)} \rightarrow \text{Ba(s)} + 3\text{N}_2\text{(g)}$$

We start with a solid reactant and end with a solid product plus a gaseous product. This change in state is the most important factor for determining entropy.

steps.title: Compare gas moles
steps.content:
Next, we count the number of moles of gas on each side of the equation. Since gas particles are much more spread out and move more freely than solids, they have significantly higher entropy.

- **Reactants:** $0$ moles of gas (only $1$ mole of solid).
- **Products:** $3$ moles of gas (plus $1$ mole of solid).

There is a net increase of $3$ moles of gas as the reaction proceeds. This is covered in **Topic R1.4: Entropy and spontaneity**.

steps.title: Relate gas to disorder
steps.content: Because the reaction produces $3$ moles of nitrogen gas from a solid reactant, the system's particles become much more spread out and have many more possible arrangements. This results in a significant increase in the disorder of the system.

steps.title: Predict entropy change sign
steps.content:
Since the disorder of the system increases, the entropy change is positive.

$$\Delta S > 0$$

In an IB exam, you earn one mark for stating the sign and another for justifying it by mentioning the formation of gas from a solid.

steps.title: Identify thermodynamic signs
steps.content:
To solve this using the Gibbs Free Energy Relationship, we first need to identify the signs of $\Delta H$ and $\Delta G$ from the information provided in the question:

1. The reaction is **endothermic**, which means the enthalpy change is positive ($ \Delta H > 0 $).
2. The reaction is **spontaneous**, which means the Gibbs free energy change is negative ($ \Delta G < 0 $).

steps.title: Apply Gibbs Free Energy equation
steps.content:
We use the Gibbs free energy equation found in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

Where:
- $T$ is the absolute temperature in Kelvin (which is always a positive value).
- $\Delta S$ is the change in entropy.

steps.title: Deduce the sign of entropy
steps.content:
Let's look at the relationship between the signs. For a reaction to be spontaneous (negative $\Delta G$) despite being endothermic (positive $\Delta H$), the term $-T\Delta S$ must be negative and large enough to outweigh the positive $\Delta H$.

$$\text{Negative } \Delta G = (\text{Positive } \Delta H) - (\text{Positive } T \times \Delta S)$$

Since $T$ is always positive, the only way for the term $-T\Delta S$ to be negative is if **$\Delta S$ is positive** ($ \Delta S > 0 $).

steps.title: Provide molecular justification
steps.content:
Finally, we justify this prediction by looking at the states of matter in the balanced equation:

$$\text{Ba(N}_3)_2\text{(s)} \rightarrow \text{Ba(s)} + 3\text{N}_2\text{(g)}$$

Notice that we start with 1 mole of a **solid** reactant and produce 1 mole of a solid and **3 moles of gas**. Gases have much higher entropy (disorder) than solids. The significant increase in the number of gas molecules means the system becomes much more disordered, confirming that the entropy change is positive.

parts.content: State the Gibbs free energy equation and the condition under which a reaction is spontaneous.
parts.markscheme:
- State the Gibbs free energy equation: $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. <marks>A1</marks>
- State that a reaction is spontaneous when $\Delta G < 0$. <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Recall the Gibbs equation
steps.content:
To understand why some reactions proceed without external energy, we use the Gibbs free energy equation. This equation relates the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$), the absolute temperature ($T$ in Kelvin), and the entropy change ($\Delta S$). 

You can find the standard version of this equation in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet.

steps.title: State the equation
steps.content:
For the first mark, you simply need to state the relationship. While the data booklet provides the standard version, the general form is:

$$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$$

steps.title: Define the spontaneity condition
steps.content:
A reaction is considered "spontaneous" if it can occur on its own under a given set of conditions. In thermodynamics, this happens when the change in Gibbs free energy is negative. 

Condition for spontaneity:
$$\Delta G < 0$$

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn both marks on this question:
1. State the equation: **$\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$** (1 mark)
2. State the condition: **$\Delta G < 0$** (1 mark)

This is a fundamental concept in **Topic R1.4: Entropy and spontaneity**. If you're unsure how $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ compete to determine spontaneity, reviewing that chapter will be very helpful!

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, the sign of the standard entropy change ($\Delta S^\ominus$) for this reaction.
parts.markscheme: Negative AND there is a decrease in the number of moles of gas (from 3 moles of reactants to 2 moles of products) / decrease in disorder. <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the standard enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\ominus$) for the reaction in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta H^\ominus = \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum \Delta H_f^\ominus(\text{reactants})$
$\Delta H^\ominus = 2(-395.7) - [2(-296.8) + 0] = -197.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content: Calculate the standard entropy change ($\Delta S^\ominus$) for the reaction in $\text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta S^\ominus = \sum S^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum S^\ominus(\text{reactants})$

$\Delta S^\ominus = 2(256.8) - [2(248.2) + 205.2] = -188.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

parts.content:
Determine the temperature, in K, at which the reaction becomes non-spontaneous. Assume that $\Delta H^\ominus$ and $\Delta S^\ominus$ are independent of temperature.

If you did not obtain answers in Part 2 and Part 3, use $-190.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ and $-180.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$ respectively, but these are not the correct answers.
parts.markscheme:
Reaction becomes non-spontaneous when $\Delta G^\ominus = 0$, so $T = \frac{\Delta H^\ominus}{\Delta S^\ominus}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$T = \frac{-197800\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{-188.0\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}} = 1052\ \text{K}$

Accept $1050\ \text{K}$ to $1053\ \text{K}$ depending on rounding. <marks>1 mark</marks>

If ECF values used: $T = \frac{-190000}{-180.0} = 1056\ \text{K}$

parts.content: Calculate the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\ominus$), in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, for this reaction at $1100\ \text{K}$.
parts.markscheme:
$\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus$

$\Delta G^\ominus = -197.8\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} - (1100\ \text{K} \times -0.1880\ \text{kJ K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1})$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

$\Delta G^\ominus = +9.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>

If ECF values from Part 4 placeholder used: $\Delta G^\ominus = -190.0 - (1100 \times -0.1800) = +8.0\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$

parts.content: Predict, with a reason, whether the value of the equilibrium constant ($K$) will be greater than or less than 1 at $1100\ \text{K}$.
parts.markscheme: $K < 1$ AND $\Delta G^\ominus$ is positive / reaction is non-spontaneous (so $\ln K$ must be negative). <marks>1 mark</marks>

options.markscheme: For this reaction, gas moles decrease from 4 to 2 so $\Delta S < 0$ (not $>0$). Also $\Delta G=\Delta H-T\Delta S$ with $\Delta H<0$ and $\Delta S<0$ is not spontaneous at all $T$.

Gas moles decrease (4 to 2), so $\Delta S<0$. Formation of $\text{NH}_3$ is exothermic, so $\Delta H<0$. With $\Delta H<0$ and $\Delta S<0$, $\Delta G=\Delta H-T\Delta S$ is more likely negative at low $T$, so spontaneity is favored at low $T$.

While $\Delta S<0$ is consistent, the reaction is exothermic so $\Delta H$ is not $>0$. Also $\Delta H>0$ and $\Delta S<0$ would indeed imply $\Delta G>0$ at all $T$, but the $\Delta H$ sign is incorrect for this process.
For this reaction, gas moles decrease from 4 to 2 so $\Delta S<0$ (not $>0$). The reaction is also exothermic ($\Delta H<0$), not endothermic.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Spontaneity depends on Gibbs free energy (ΔG = ΔH − TΔS), so temperature (T) is the additional factor alongside ΔH and ΔS.

options.markscheme: Bond enthalpies can be used to estimate the enthalpy change ($\Delta H$) of a reaction, but they do not serve as an additional independent factor in the Gibbs free energy equation.

While pressure can influence the Gibbs free energy of gaseous systems under non-standard conditions, it is not the additional factor used alongside $\Delta H$ and $\Delta S$ in the equation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$.

The spontaneity of a reaction (under standard conditions) is determined by the Gibbs free energy change, given by the equation $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$. Therefore, temperature ($T$) is the additional factor that, together with enthalpy and entropy, determines spontaneity.

Concentration affects the reaction quotient ($Q$) and therefore the non-standard Gibbs free energy, but it is not one of the variables in the stated standard-condition relationship $\Delta G = \Delta H - T\Delta S$.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: The question asks which reaction is spontaneous at a given temperature using ΔH°, ΔS°, and T, which requires applying ΔG = ΔH − TΔS and the criterion ΔG < 0 for spontaneity.

options.markscheme: For reaction A: $\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus = -50 - (600 \times -0.100) = -50 + 60 = +10 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. Since $\Delta G^\ominus > 0$, the reaction is non-spontaneous.
For reaction B: $\Delta G^\ominus = 50 - (300 \times -0.100) = 50 + 30 = +80 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. Since $\Delta G^\ominus > 0$, the reaction is non-spontaneous.

For reaction C: $\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus = 50 - (600 \times 0.100) = 50 - 60 = -10 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. Since $\Delta G^\ominus$ is negative, the reaction is spontaneous at this temperature. This can also be interpreted as the total entropy change being positive: $\Delta S_{\text{total}} = \Delta S_{\text{sys}} + \Delta S_{\text{surr}} = +100 + (-\frac{50000}{600}) \approx +16.7 \text{ J}\,\text{K}^{-1}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$.
For reaction D: $\Delta G^\ominus = 50 - (400 \times 0.100) = 50 - 40 = +10 \text{ kJ}\,\text{mol}^{-1}$. Since $\Delta G^\ominus > 0$, the reaction is non-spontaneous.

parts.content: Calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change, $\Delta G^\circ$, at $700\ \mathrm{K}$.
parts.markscheme:
Convert $\Delta S^\circ$ to $\mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$:
$\Delta S^\circ = -188\ \mathrm{J\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}} = -0.188\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}\ K^{-1}}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

Use $\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$:
$\Delta G^\circ = -197 - (700 \times -0.188)$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

$\Delta G^\circ = -65.4\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the relevant formula
steps.content:
To calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$), we need the formula that links enthalpy ($\Delta H^\circ$), entropy ($\Delta S^\circ$), and temperature ($T$). You can find this in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your IB Data Booklet.

$$\Delta G^\circ = \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ$$

This concept is a key part of **Topic R1.4: Entropy and spontaneity (HL)**. If you need a refresher on how energy drives reactions, that's the place to look!

steps.title: Convert entropy to kJ
steps.content:
Before we plug numbers into the formula, we have a unit mismatch! $\Delta H^\circ$ is in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, but $\Delta S^\circ$ is given in $\text{J mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$. 

To keep everything consistent in kiloJoules (kJ), we divide the entropy value by $1000$:

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{-188\text{ J mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}}{1000} = -0.188\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$$

Converting this unit correctly is worth the first mark in the markscheme.

calculator.description: Convert J to kJ by dividing by 1000.

steps.title: Substitute and solve
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the known values into the Gibbs free energy equation. Remember to use the temperature in Kelvin ($700\text{ K}$) and be careful with the double negatives!

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -197 - (700 \times -0.188) \end{aligned}$$

First, calculate the $T\Delta S^\circ$ term:
$$700 \times -0.188 = -131.6\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Then subtract it from $\Delta H^\circ$:
$$\Delta G^\circ = -197 - (-131.6) = -197 + 131.6 = -65.4\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

calculator.description: Calculate the final Gibbs free energy change.

steps.title: Final answer
steps.content:
The standard Gibbs free energy change at $700\text{ K}$ is:

$$\Delta G^\circ = -65.4\text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Since $\Delta G^\circ$ is negative, this tells us the reaction is spontaneous under these standard conditions at $700\text{ K}$.

steps.title: Identify the formula
steps.content:
To calculate the standard Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$), we use the relationship between enthalpy ($\Delta H^\circ$), temperature ($T$), and entropy ($\Delta S^\circ$). This formula is found in your IB Data Booklet.

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

steps.title: Convert Enthalpy to Joules
steps.content:
Notice that $\Delta H^\circ$ is given in $\text{kJ}$ while $\Delta S^\circ$ is in $\text{J}$. In this method, we will convert everything to the base unit of **Joules** first. 

$$\Delta H^\circ = -197\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \times 1000 = -197,000\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Substitute and Solve (Joules)
steps.content:
Now we substitute our values ($T = 700\ \text{K}$, $\Delta S^\circ = -188\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}$, and our new $\Delta H^\circ$) into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta G^\circ &= -197,000 - (700 \times -188) \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -197,000 - (-131,600) \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -65,400\ \text{J mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type the expression -197000 - (700 * -188) and press Enter.

desmos.instructions: Input the calculation directly into the bar.

calculator.description: Calculate the Gibbs free energy in Joules.

steps.title: Convert back to kJ
steps.content:
Final answers for $\Delta G^\circ$ are typically expected in $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. To finish, divide your result by $1000$:

$$\Delta G^\circ = \frac{-65,400\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{1000} = -65.4\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$$

This negative value indicates that the reaction is spontaneous at $700\ \text{K}$, as taught in **Topic R1.4 Entropy and Spontaneity**.

parts.content: Determine whether the reaction is spontaneous at this temperature.
parts.markscheme:
Yes; $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ so the reaction is spontaneous.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Select the relevant formula
steps.content:
To determine if a reaction is spontaneous, we need to find the value of the Gibbs free energy change ($\Delta G^\circ$). We can find the relationship between enthalpy, temperature, and entropy in the **Key Chemistry Equations** section of your data booklet.

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

steps.title: Convert entropy units
steps.content:
Before calculating, check your units! The enthalpy ($\Delta H^\circ$) is given in **kJ**, while the entropy ($\Delta S^\circ$) is in **J**. 

To make them consistent, we convert $\Delta S^\circ$ to $\text{kJ mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$ by dividing by $1000$:

$$\Delta S^\circ = \frac{-188}{1000} = -0.188\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}\text{ K}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Calculate Gibbs free energy
steps.content:
Now, substitute the temperature ($700\ \text{K}$) and the other values into the equation:

$$\begin{aligned} \Delta G^\circ &= \Delta H^\circ - T\Delta S^\circ \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -197 - (700 \times -0.188) \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -197 + 131.6 \\ \Delta G^\circ &= -65.4\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Type the expression to find the result.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expression directly into the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Enter the formula using the given variables.

calculator.description: Calculate the value of Delta G

steps.title: State the conclusion
steps.content:
In chemistry, a reaction is spontaneous if the change in Gibbs free energy is negative ($\Delta G^\circ < 0$). 

Since our calculated value is $-65.4\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, the reaction is **spontaneous** at $700\ \text{K}$.

steps.title: Convert units to Joules
steps.content:
To perform calculations with both enthalpy and entropy, we must ensure their units match. Typically, we convert the enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\circ$) from $\text{kJ}$ to $\text{J}$.

$$\Delta H^\circ = -197\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1} \times 1000 = -197,000\ \text{J mol}^{-1}$$

steps.title: Identify crossover conditions
steps.content:
The "crossover temperature" is the specific temperature where a reaction changes from being non-spontaneous to spontaneous (or vice versa). At this point, the system is at equilibrium and the Gibbs free energy change is zero:

$$\Delta G^\circ = 0$$

From the Data Booklet, we use the Gibbs free energy equation:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus}=\Delta H^{\ominus}-T \Delta S^{\ominus}$$

Setting $\Delta G^\circ = 0$ and rearranging for temperature ($T$), we get:

$$T_{\text{crossover}} = \frac{\Delta H^\circ}{\Delta S^\circ}$$

steps.title: Calculate crossover temperature
steps.content:
Now, substitute the values for enthalpy and entropy into the rearranged equation to find the threshold temperature:

$$T_{\text{crossover}} = \frac{-197,000\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{-188\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}} \approx 1048\ \text{K}$$

gdc.instructions: Divide -197000 by -188 in the calculation menu.

calculator.description: Calculate the crossover temperature by dividing enthalpy by entropy.

steps.title: Determine spontaneous temperature range
steps.content:
For this reaction, both $\Delta H^\circ$ and $\Delta S^\circ$ are negative. 

1. The negative enthalpy (exothermic) favors spontaneity.
2. The negative entropy (becoming more ordered) opposes spontaneity as temperature increases.

In this "negative-negative" scenario, the reaction is spontaneous at **low temperatures** (where the enthalpy term $|\Delta H^\circ|$ is larger than the entropy term $|T\Delta S^\circ|$). This means the reaction is spontaneous for all temperatures below the crossover temperature:

$$\text{Spontaneous if: } T < 1048\ \text{K}$$

steps.title: Final comparison and conclusion
steps.content:
The reaction takes place at $700\ \text{K}$. Since $700\ \text{K}$ is less than the crossover temperature of $1048\ \text{K}$, the reaction is spontaneous.

$$700\ \text{K} < 1048\ \text{K}$$

This matches the markscheme requirement that $\Delta G^\circ < 0$ at this temperature.

highlights.text: Yes; $\Delta G^⊖ < 0$ so the reaction is spontaneous.

parts.content: Suggest one reason why this process is carried out at $700\ \mathrm{K}$ despite the negative enthalpy change.
parts.markscheme:
Compromise temperature: sufficient rate of reaction while maintaining acceptable yield.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Analyze the thermodynamic yield
steps.content:
First, we look at the enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\circ$). The question states that $\Delta H^\circ = -197\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$, which means the reaction is **exothermic**. 

According to **Le Châtelier’s Principle** (covered in **Topic R2.3.4**), a decrease in temperature favors the exothermic direction to counteract the change. Therefore, to get the maximum equilibrium yield of $SO_3$, we would ideally want a very low temperature.

steps.title: Consider the kinetic rate
steps.content:
While low temperatures increase the yield, they significantly decrease the **rate of reaction**. 

Based on **Collision Theory** (**Topic R2.2.2**), as temperature decreases, particles have less kinetic energy. Fewer particles will have energy greater than or equal to the activation energy ($E_a$), and the frequency of successful collisions drops. In an industrial setting like the Contact Process, a reaction that takes years to reach equilibrium is not useful, even if the yield is high.

steps.title: Identify the compromise
steps.content:
To solve this conflict between thermodynamics (yield) and kinetics (rate), chemical engineers use a **compromise temperature**.

At $700\ \mathrm{K}$:
1. The temperature is **high enough** to provide a sufficient **rate of reaction** so that $SO_3$ is produced quickly.
2. The temperature is **low enough** to ensure an **acceptable equilibrium yield** (as the reaction is still spontaneous with $\Delta G^\circ \approx -65.4\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$).

calculator.description: Calculate Gibbs Free Energy at 700K to verify spontaneity.

steps.title: Final Answer Justification
steps.content:
In an exam, you should clearly state that the temperature is a balance of two factors. 

**Final Answer:** $700\ \mathrm{K}$ is a compromise temperature that provides a sufficient rate of reaction while maintaining an acceptable equilibrium yield.

steps.title: Analyze the thermodynamic conflict
steps.content:
The reaction is exothermic, as shown by the negative enthalpy change. According to Le Châtelier’s principle, a lower temperature would favor the forward reaction and increase the yield of $\mathrm{SO}_3$. 

However, in industrial chemistry, we must balance the **yield** (how much we get) with the **rate** (how fast we get it). If the temperature is too low, the reaction happens too slowly to be profitable.

steps.title: Link temperature to energy
steps.content: Temperature is a measure of the average kinetic energy of the particles. According to **Topic S1.1.3: Temperature and Kinetic Energy**, decreasing the temperature reduces the energy available to the reactant molecules ($\mathrm{SO}_2$ and $\mathrm{O}_2$).

steps.title: Consider the activation energy
steps.content:
For a collision to result in a reaction, the colliding particles must have energy greater than or equal to the **activation energy** ($E_a$). 

At lower temperatures, a much smaller fraction of particles possesses $E \geq E_a$. We can see this relationship in the Arrhenius equation from the data booklet:

$$\begin{aligned} k &= A e^{\frac{-E_{a}}{RT}} \end{aligned}$$

As temperature ($T$) decreases, the rate constant ($k$) decreases exponentially, leading to a much slower rate of reaction.

steps.title: Justify the industrial choice
steps.content:
The process is carried out at $700\ \mathrm{K}$ to ensure a **sufficient rate of reaction**. 

At this "compromise temperature," enough particles have sufficient kinetic energy to overcome the activation energy barrier, allowing the system to reach equilibrium quickly enough for industrial viability, even if the theoretical maximum yield is slightly lower than it would be at room temperature.

parts.content: Explain how increasing temperature further would affect the spontaneity of this reaction.
parts.markscheme:
Higher temperature increases the magnitude of the $T\Delta S^\circ$ term (making $-T\Delta S^\circ$ more positive).
Since $\Delta S^\circ$ is negative, $\Delta G^\circ$ becomes less negative (or more positive), decreasing spontaneity.
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the spontaneity equation
steps.content:
To determine how spontaneity changes with temperature, we use the Gibbs free energy equation from the data booklet:

$$\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} - T\Delta S^{\ominus}$$

Recall from **Topic R1.4 (Entropy and spontaneity)** that a reaction is spontaneous when $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ is negative. The more negative $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ is, the more spontaneous the reaction.

steps.title: Analyze the entropy term
steps.content:
From the question, we know that the entropy change is negative:
$$\Delta S^{\ominus} = -188\ \text{J mol}^{-1}\ \text{K}^{-1}$$

In the equation $\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} - T\Delta S^{\ominus}$, consider the term $-T\Delta S^{\ominus}$. Because $\Delta S^{\ominus}$ is negative, multiplying it by $-T$ (where $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin and always positive) results in a **positive** value.

steps.title: Evaluate increasing temperature
steps.content: When we increase the temperature $T$, the magnitude of the $T\Delta S^{\ominus}$ term increases. Since $\Delta S^{\ominus}$ is negative, the term $-T\Delta S^{\ominus}$ becomes **more positive** as $T$ gets larger.

steps.title: Determine effect on spontaneity
steps.content:
Since $\Delta G^{\ominus} = \Delta H^{\ominus} + (\text{a more positive term})$, the value of $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ will become **less negative** (or more positive). 

As $\Delta G^{\ominus}$ moves towards zero or becomes positive, the reaction becomes **less spontaneous**. This explains why the Contact Process is typically carried out at a compromise temperature rather than a very high one!

steps.title: Define spontaneity via entropy
steps.content:
In the total entropy approach, a reaction is spontaneous if the total entropy change of the universe ($
\Delta S_{\text{total}}$) is positive. This is calculated by adding the entropy change of the system and the surroundings:

$$\Delta S_{\text{total}} = \Delta S_{\text{system}} + \Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}$$

steps.title: Identify entropy of surroundings
steps.content:
The entropy change of the surroundings is determined by the heat transferred to or from the system at a specific temperature ($T$):

$$\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = -\frac{\Delta H}{T}$$

Given that the reaction is exothermic, $\Delta H = -197\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, the entropy change of the surroundings is positive because heat is released into the environment, increasing its disorder.

steps.title: Analyze temperature effect
steps.content:
As the temperature ($T$) increases, the value of the positive term $\frac{-\Delta H}{T}$ decreases because the denominator is getting larger. 

At $700\ \text{K}$, we can verify the values:

$$\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}} = \frac{197000\ \text{J mol}^{-1}}{700\ \text{K}} \approx +281.4\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$$

If temperature increases (e.g., to $1000\ \text{K}$), this value drops to $197\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 197000 / 700 and 197000 / 1000 in the main calculation menu.

ti84.instructions: Enter 197000 / 700 to find the entropy at 700K. Then enter 197000 / 1000 to see how it changes at 1000K.

desmos.instructions: Type 197000 / 700 and 197000 / 1000 into separate lines to compare the results.

calculator.description: Calculate surrounding entropy at 700K and 1000K

steps.title: Evaluate total spontaneity
steps.content:
Since $\Delta S_{\text{system}}$ is negative ($-188\ \text{J K}^{-1} \text{mol}^{-1}$), and the positive contribution from $\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}$ is decreasing as temperature rises, the sum $\Delta S_{\text{total}}$ becomes less positive (smaller).

Eventually, at high enough temperatures, $\Delta S_{\text{surroundings}}$ will no longer be large enough to outweigh the negative $\Delta S_{\text{system}}$, and $\Delta S_{\text{total}}$ will become negative. Therefore, **increasing temperature decreases the spontaneity** of the reaction.

steps.title: Identify the reaction type
steps.content: First, we look at the standard enthalpy change ($\Delta H^\circ$) provided in the question. Since $\Delta H^\circ$ is negative ($-197\ \mathrm{kJ\ mol^{-1}}$), we know that the forward reaction is exothermic. In an exothermic reaction, heat can be thought of as a "product" of the reaction.

steps.title: Apply Le Chatelier's Principle
steps.content:
According to **Le Chatelier’s Principle** (which you can review in **Topic R2.3**), if a system at equilibrium is subjected to a change in temperature, the system will shift to partially oppose that change. 

Because the forward reaction is exothermic, increasing the temperature adds "heat" to the system. To counteract this, the equilibrium position will shift in the endothermic direction (the reverse direction) to absorb the extra heat.

steps.title: Relate shift to spontaneity
steps.content:
When the equilibrium position shifts toward the reactants, the forward direction becomes less favored. 

In chemistry, if the formation of products becomes less favored as temperature increases, it indicates that the reaction is becoming **less spontaneous**. Therefore, increasing the temperature further makes the production of $\mathrm{SO}_3$ less spontaneous.

labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Conclusions about how equilibrium shifts and how K changes with temperature (temperature dependence of K) are central to the options.

options.content: The equilibrium position shifts to the right as temperature increases.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. Since $\Delta G^\ominus$ increases with temperature, the slope ($-\Delta S^\ominus$) is positive, meaning $\Delta S^\ominus$ is negative. Using $\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus$, we find $\Delta H^\ominus$ is negative (exothermic). For exothermic reactions, the equilibrium shifts left as temperature increases.

options.content: The forward reaction becomes more spontaneous above $335\text{ K}$.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. $\Delta G^\ominus$ becomes less negative as temperature increases: at $335\ \text{K}$, $\Delta G^\ominus=-2.1\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$, and at $375\ \text{K}$, $\Delta G^\ominus=+9.1\ \text{kJ mol}^{-1}$. Therefore the forward reaction becomes less spontaneous as temperature increases (and is not spontaneous at $375\ \text{K}$).

options.content: The standard entropy of the reactants is higher than that of the products.
options.markscheme: Correct. From the data, $\Delta G^\ominus$ increases as $T$ increases. In the equation $\Delta G^\ominus = \Delta H^\ominus - T\Delta S^\ominus$, the slope of a $\Delta G^\ominus$ vs $T$ plot is $-\Delta S^\ominus$. A positive slope implies $-\Delta S^\ominus > 0$, hence $\Delta S^\ominus < 0$. If the entropy change is negative, the entropy of the reactants is higher than the entropy of the products.

options.content: The equilibrium constant ($K_{\text{c}}$) increases as temperature increases.
options.markscheme: Incorrect. As $\Delta G^\ominus$ increases (becomes more positive), the equilibrium constant decreases according to $\Delta G^\ominus = -RT\ln K_{\text{c}}$.

options.content: Entropy is a measure of the energy content of a system.
The entropy of a perfect crystal is zero at absolute zero temperature ($0\text{ K}$).
A decrease in entropy always indicates a spontaneous process.
The units of entropy are typically expressed in joules per mole per kelvin ($\text{J\,mol}^{-1}\,\text{K}^{-1}$).

options.markscheme: Incorrect. This value is obtained by failing to convert $\Delta H^\ominus$ from $\text{kJ}$ to $\text{J}$, leading to an answer that is a factor of $1000$ too small.

Correct. First, calculate $\Delta S^\ominus$: $\Delta S^\ominus = \sum S^\ominus(\text{products}) - \sum S^\ominus(\text{reactants}) = (2 \times 240) - 120 = 360 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}$. Spontaneity occurs when $\Delta G < 0$. At the threshold where $\Delta G = 0$, $T = \frac{\Delta H^\ominus}{\Delta S^\ominus} = \frac{60000 \text{ J mol}^{-1}}{360 \text{ J K}^{-1} \text{ mol}^{-1}} \approx 167 \text{ K}$.
Incorrect. This calculation ($\frac{60000}{240}$) ignores the entropy of the reactant and the stoichiometric coefficient of the product.

options.content: It is spontaneous at all temperatures.
options.markscheme: Not correct: with $\Delta H^\theta<0$ and $\Delta S^\theta<0$, $\Delta G^\theta=\Delta H^\theta-T\Delta S^\theta$ becomes less favourable as $T$ increases, so it is not spontaneous at all temperatures.

options.content: It is spontaneous only at low temperatures.
options.markscheme: Correct: $\Delta H^\theta<0$ and $\Delta S^\theta<0$ so $\Delta G^\theta=\Delta H^\theta-T\Delta S^\theta$ is negative only at sufficiently low $T$ (as $-T\Delta S^\theta$ is positive and increases with $T$).

options.content: It is spontaneous only at high temperatures.

options.content: It is non-spontaneous at any temperature.
options.markscheme: Not correct: at low $T$, the negative $\Delta H^\theta$ term can dominate so $\Delta G^\theta$ can be negative, meaning the reaction can be spontaneous at sufficiently low temperatures.
